narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 65.9% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile shows solid aspects, like being a full-time student from an upper-middle-income family, identifying as a white male—traits common among applicants—but it also highlights some challenges in your academic record. Specifically, your undergraduate GPA of 2.60 falls below the typical 3.21 average, your LSAT score of 34 is under the usual 36.7, and your school decile ranks are on the lower end: 3rd in Year 1 (versus an average of about 6th) and 2nd in Year 3 (well below the typical 6th place). When the model weighed these, your Year 3 decile rank had the strongest influence, pushing the prediction toward failure because it's unusually low compared to most students who pass. Next in impact was your Year 1 decile, also signaling weaker early performance relative to peers. Your LSAT score ranked third in influence, as its below-average mark similarly nudged the odds downward. Family income followed, with upper-middle status not providing the boost seen in some higher brackets, then your undergraduate GPA reinforced the concern with its dip below the norm. Your racial background and gender had milder effects but still tilted slightly toward the riskier side in this context, while full-time status was the least influential. Overall, these factors combined to outweigh any positives, unlike typical successful applicants who cluster higher in grades and test scores. The good news is several of these are changeable: retaking the LSAT to aim above 36 could make a big difference, as could building a stronger GPA through post-bac courses or explaining context in a new application. Focusing on grade improvement strategies now might also shift your deciles in future evaluations. We encourage reapplying after addressing these—many students turn predictions around with targeted effort. Hang in there; this is just one data point, and your determination can rewrite the story.
